content:
I'm a master student and it was just decided that for my semester project I will be working on rewriting a C driver in Rust. The driver works but hasn't been upstreamed yet, so the "no duplicate drivers" rule doesn't prevent my Rust rewrite from being upstreamed.

It's a well-known problem that upstreaming Rust drivers is a bit of a chicken-and-egg problem: APIs need users to be upstreamed and drivers need their APIs to be upstreamed. This means several unrelated tasks need to be completed and upstreamed at once.

An idea my advisor and I had was to upstream a version of the Rust driver that simply calls unsafe, generated bindings to the C APIs. This would be much easier because safe API wrappers in Rust don't have to be upstreamed at the same time. After that, safe API wrappers can be upstreamed more easily because a potential users already exists in-tree.

I'm assuming this approach has already been considered. What was the conclusion on that? Is there a rule against using unsafe in Rust drivers? Or is it a bad idea for other reasons?

I would not advice having direct unsafe calls to C code in your driver. I think there are two viable approoaches.

1) Submit your driver as an RFC. Base the patches on a git tree that includes abstractions for the subsystems your driver interfaces. Then start upstreaming the subsystem abstractions, pointing the RFC for your driver as the user. This is the approach taken by rust binder.

2) Submit your driver as a stub to be included in tree. Then submit your subsystem abstractions little by little, pointing to your in-tree stub driver as the user. When subsystem abstractions are merged, grow your stub driver to use them. This is the approach taken by nova.
Rust null block is also taking approach 2.
That's very helpful, thank you!
I am curious about your driver. What does it enable? :)
Which abstractions would be needed for your driver?

The exact driver is not set in stone yet, I could either do our FPD-Link driver or the one for a Lontium chip for 4k60 video output.

More likely the FPD-Link one, because the Lontium driver needs the v4l2 abstractions and the nvidia hardware we use it on needs out-of-tree patches in the v4l2 code. Which might be annoying to deal with / reduce the chances of producing upstreamable work.

Concerning the abstractions necessary: I'm actually not familiar with these two drivers yet myself. The choice was made based on discussions with collegues. For now I can only look at the include statements:

// FPD-Link
#include <linux/gpio.h>
#include <linux/delay.h>
#include <linux/i2c.h>
#include <linux/io.h>
#include <linux/kernel.h>
#include <linux/media.h>
#include <linux/module.h>
#include <linux/of.h>
#include <linux/of_device.h>
#include <linux/of_gpio.h>
#include <linux/regmap.h>

// Lontium
#include <linux/module.h>
#include <linux/i2c.h>
#include <linux/of_device.h>
#include <linux/of_gpio.h>
#include <linux/interrupt.h>
#include <linux/v4l2-dv-timings.h>
#include <media/v4l2-dv-timings.h>
#include <media/v4l2-ctrls.h>
#include <media/v4l2-event.h>
#include <media/camera_common.h>
#ifdef CONFIG_V4L2_FWNODE
#include <media/v4l2-fwnode.h>
#else
#include <media/v4l2-of.h>
#endif
#include "lt6911uxc_regs.h"

For FPD-Link, I see the following that have something available but not upstream yet:

  • GPIO Consumer (pretty basic for now): https://github.com/Fabo/linux/commit/fecb4bd73f06bb2cac8e16aca7ef0e2f1b6acb50
  • Regmap: https://github.com/Fabo/linux/commit/ec0b740ac5ab299e4c86011a0002919e5bbe5c2d
  • I2C: https://github.com/Fabo/linux/commit/70ed30fcdf8ec62fa91485c3c0a161a9d0194668
  • OF
  • Delay

This would definitely be the easiest one to do.
Thanks a lot!!

• OF: https://github.com/Fabo/linux/pull/2/commits/72af582719d794fbb5c909ee3af6fedde7448d96 (at least this is the best I was able to find so far)
  • Delay: https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/pull/920 nearly two years old, but should be a starting point :grinning: (taken from the RFL Patch & Project Registry)
Hrtimer should give delay capability soon enough

Remo Senekowitsch said:

I could either do our FPD-Link driver

Having a quick look to that I would think using Fabien's rust-ncv6336 branch would be a good starting point. With that you would get I2C and regmap "for free". Then put some delay/hrtimer support, OF support (please let me know if you like me to rebase that on Fabien's recent work) and maybe some sysfs support on top of it. And then I would think you are good to start with your driver work :grinning:
Thank you all for the awesome support! I'm very new to kernel development so I need some time to look at all these patches.

I had been using the following for delay in my other driver: https://github.com/Fabo/linux/commit/900f24f5bd97597accf476270703bb60629953a7. Could use that while waiting for hrtimer to gain delay capability.

Sorry for the extremely late reply. I got distracted with other work, but I also started getting familiar with the build and config system. Now I'm honestly struggling with applying / managing these multiple patches. Maybe it has already been discussed? In that case, I would be very thankful for a link. How do other people do it? I have multiple remotes now, trying to merge branches across them (while they are being rebased regularly), getting merge conflicts with stuff that is seemingly unrelated to the patch I want to pull in... Should I cherry-pick individual commits instead? I don't mind doing this rebase work if it is actually necessary, but something tells me I'm doing something wrong and just wasting time. I also haven't figured out how to apply a patch series from the mailing list archives, but that feels like something I'll be able to figure out on my own. Any help is appreciated :)

Remo Senekowitsch said:

Sorry for the extremely late reply. I got distracted with other work, but I also started getting familiar with the build and config system. Now I'm honestly struggling with applying / managing these multiple patches. Maybe it has already been discussed? In that case, I would be very thankful for a link. How do other people do it? I have multiple remotes now, trying to merge branches across them (while they are being rebased regularly), getting merge conflicts with stuff that is seemingly unrelated to the patch I want to pull in... Should I cherry-pick individual commits instead? I don't mind doing this rebase work if it is actually necessary, but something tells me I'm doing something wrong and just wasting time. I also haven't figured out how to apply a patch series from the mailing list archives, but that feels like something I'll be able to figure out on my own. Any help is appreciated :)

At the moment I think the best choice for you might be Fabien's b4/ncv6336 branch. It has Danilo's work which will be merged to v6.14 and Fabien's work which was recently sent to the mailing list for review. So you have both parts in that branch without the need to pick it here and there. I'd propose that you have a look to that and check if that would be a starting point for you. And maybe what is missing.

Regarding applying patch series from the mailing list a lot of people are using the b4 tool for that.
Oh, fantastic. That one applies without any conflicts. Thanks a lot.

I need to read some properties from the device tree to configure the driver. (The C driver uses of_property_read_u32.) I don't think this exists on the b4/ncv6336 branch, so I searched elsewhere. (The RFL patch registry is 8 months old, is there a better source now?) I found this patch for the AsahiLinux GPU driver, which seems to have what I need. It's almost two years old though, is it a good idea to try to build on top of that?

Remo Senekowitsch said:

I need to read some properties from the device tree to configure the driver. (The C driver uses of_property_read_u32.) I don't think this exists on the b4/ncv6336 branch, so I searched elsewhere. (The RFL patch registry is 8 months old, is there a better source now?) I found this patch for the AsahiLinux GPU driver, which seems to have what I need. It's almost two years old though, is it a good idea to try to build on top of that?

It's actually the "device property" API we want to abstract (at least at first). I've sent out a v1[1]. I've not yet gotten around to sending out v2, but it is on a branch here[2].

[1] https://lore.kernel.org/all/20241025-rust-platform-dev-v1-0-0df8dcf7c20b@kernel.org/
[2] https://git.kernel.org/pub/scm/linux/kernel/git/robh/linux.git/log/?h=rust/device-properties
Thanks, I will try to work off of that.

Reading properties works well! But now I also have some nesting going on in my device tree. Is it possible to read that via the "device property" API? I can only find functions that read scalar values (and arrays). My device tree looks roughly like this:

fpdlink: ds90ub954@30 {
    compatible = "ti,ds90ub954"; // matches driver

    // ...scalar values, I can read these with "device property" API

    serializers {
        // ...

        serializer@0 { /**/ };
        serializer@1 { /**/ };
        serializer@2 { /**/ };
        // ...more serializers...
    };
};

The C driver uses of_get_child_by_name and of_get_child_by_name to parse these nested nodes.

Remo Senekowitsch said:

I also have some nesting going on in my device tree. Is it possible to read that via the "device property" API?

As far as I understand @Rob Herring's rust: Add bindings for reading device properties commit message

next up is child node accessors/iterators

nesting is not supported, yet.

Thanks, I missed that. Is there a plan for what APIs the child node accessors/iterators are going to be based on? I might hack something together that works for me and hopefully isn't too far off from what will be upstreamed. (Ofc I'm happy to help with upstreaming if I can.)
